chunks.text: On Business Twelve Lessons from Good Dogs That Make Us Wiser at Work By Kyung Suk Yi and Jin Sung Kim © Kyung Suk Yi and Jin Sung Kim This isn’t a book about theology. Nope. It’s a book about dogs and our God they pointed us to. Through twelve simple stories, we share lessons learned from many good dogs who didn’t care much for things that didn’t matter, but always stayed close to loved ones. We are not pastors. We are one deacon and one lay person trying to love God well. These reflections are for anyone walking quietly, faithfully, still learning how to stay close to the Shepherd.

The Never Alone Mission ℅ A&K 440 N. Wolfe Rd. Sunnyvale, CA 94085 Front Cover Photo: Sydney Rae unsplash.com/@srz For those who rise early, stay late, carry more than most ever see, and still choose to work with integrity. For the quiet ones who lead without shouting, serve without seeking applause, and build things that last. For the men and women who believe business can be holy, kindness can be strong, and faith can shape the way we work. And for every good dog who reminded us what that looks like.

Introduction What dogs teach us about doing business with grace. We didn’t set out to write a business book. But the more we watched dogs and the more we walked through our own work, the more we saw something worth noticing. Dogs stay close. They show up. They forgive fast. They know when to rest. They listen well. They protect those around them. They move when the time is right. Dogs do what many of us forget in the rush of business. And in these simple things they do, they point to truths that last longer than any market cycle. Truths that have been with us since the beginning, found in the Word of God, lived out in the person of Jesus, and available to us every day in the places we work.

Whether we lead a company, clock in at a job, or run a business from the kitchen table, we are all learning how to do this well. 4 This book isn’t about management theory or leadership hacks. It’s about God’s grace and the humble, enduring strength that comes when we let the Lord lead. We hope these lessons from dogs and from Scripture will remind us that business can be both effective and kind, that excellence and compassion can live in the same room, and that walking closely with God is the best business strategy of all.

Note: The stories in this book are based on real observations of real dogs and real people, though we’ve changed names and some details to protect privacy. Some stories have been combined or adjusted to better illustrate the lessons we’ve learned. But the heart of each story, the way these dogs live and love, is absolutely true. 5

The accountant arrived at his small office every morning at 6:45 AM. Sharp. Consistent. While the trendy coffee shop next door hosted influencers and the startup on the other side burned through venture capital, he quietly did tax returns for local businesses. His yellow Lab mix followed the same routine. Every morning, the dog padded down the hallway, circled three times beside the desk chair, and lay down with a soft thump. Silent. Present. Always there. “Another exciting day?” the startup CEO joked one morning, watching the accountant unlock his door.

The accountant smiled. “Same as yesterday.” “Don’t you get bored?” The CEO’s company had just landed another million in funding. His photo was in all the tech magazines. 6 The accountant watched his dog settle into position by his feet. One satisfied tail wag, then stillness. “Not really.” Three years passed. The coffee shop changed owners twice. The startup pivoted four times before closing. Every morning at 6:45, the same door opened. The same dog took position. Clients came and went. Tax seasons rolled through.

The corner grocery owner brought her receipts in a shoebox every March. “You’re always here,” she said. “Same place. Same time. My husband’s had three accountants in ten years. They all moved to bigger things.” The accountant looked at his dog, eyes half-closed but ears alert. “We’re not going anywhere.” The dog never demanded attention. When clients arrived nervous about audits, his steady presence by the desk made the room calmer. During late nights in tax season, he stayed. Silent. Patient. There.

“Your dog doesn’t do much,” a new client observed, used to firms with marble lobbies. 7 “He shows up.” When the economy tanked, the coffee shop closed. Two more startups folded. The phone rang constantly—small business owners panicking, families worried about returns, elderly clients confused by changing laws. He answered every call. Opened at 6:45 every morning. Stayed late when needed. The dog maintained his post, a constant in chaos. Word spread through the neighborhood. The grocery owner’s nephew needed help with his food truck permits. Her neighbor’s daughter needed help with college financial aid forms. People learned: this accountant doesn’t just do taxes. He shows up when you need him.

The failed startup CEO reappeared months later, humbled. His company had folded. He needed help sorting the tax mess. “Thought you’d be in Silicon Valley by now,” he said, sitting across from the simple desk. The accountant pulled out a fresh legal pad. The dog’s tail thumped once against the floor. 8 “Still here.” As the CEO left with paperwork organized and next steps clear, he paused at the door. “I spent three years chasing the next big thing. You’ve spent three years being here.”

“Faithfulness is underrated.” That night, locking up, the accountant looked at his dog. The dog stood slowly, stretched, waited by the door. Same as every night. Ready to go home and do it again tomorrow. He thought about his grandmother’s favorite verse: “Whoever is faithful in very little is also faithful in much.” He’d built something that wouldn’t make headlines. Twenty-three small businesses counted on him being there at 6:45 AM. The grocery owner trusted him. The CEO knew where to find him when everything fell apart.

The dog walked beside him to the car, no leash needed. He knew the routine. More importantly, he knew his person. 9 This was faithfulness: showing up, staying close, building trust one ordinary day at a time. The dog did it instinctively. Humans had to choose it. Every morning. Every client. Every tax season. The accountant realized God looks for the same thing. Not the loudest or most impressive. Those who take what they’re given—a task, a team, a calling—and remain faithful with it. Those who work as if for the Lord himself.

The dog climbed into the car and settled in his spot. Tomorrow would be the same. 6:45 AM. Circle three times. Lie down. Be there. Sometimes the most powerful ministry happens in the quietest faithfulness. Sometimes just showing up changes everything. 10

The marketing director paced the conference room while the company owner sat calmly at the table, his old Labrador sleeping by his feet. “We need to move on this acquisition NOW,” the director insisted. “Three other firms are circling. If we wait, we lose everything.” The owner reached down and stroked his dog’s head. The Lab’s tail thumped once against the floor, but his eyes remained closed. “We wait,” the owner said. “Wait for what? The opportunity to pass us by?”

The owner didn’t answer immediately. He was thinking about his dog’s behavior that morning. At the dog park, a squirrel had run along the fence line. Every other dog had rushed the fence, barking frantically, running back and forth. His Lab had simply sat, watching the squirrel’s path. When it came down from the fence near them, the Lab made one focused movement toward it: calm, 11 deliberate, almost catching it before it scrambled up another tree. “My dog knows something about patience,” the owner finally said. “At the park, he never chases with the pack. He waits. Watches. Picks his moment.”

“We’re not talking about squirrels. We’re talking about a ten million dollar acquisition.” “Same principle.” The owner pulled out his phone, checking something. “That company we’re supposedly competing for? I’ve been watching them for two years. They’re desperate. Bleeding cash. Those other three firms will offer fast, offer high, offer blind.” “Exactly why we need to” “We wait.” Six weeks passed. The marketing director grew increasingly anxious. Updates flew across his desk: One firm had made an offer. Another had started due diligence. The third was arranging financing.

The owner kept saying the same thing: “We wait.” 12 His dog came to the office every day, taking the same spot by the owner’s desk. When clients arrived for meetings, the dog would lift his head, assess them, then settle back down. He observed everything from his quiet corner. The director watched this routine daily. The contrast struck him: his own frantic energy against the dog’s perfect stillness. Here he was, checking his phone every five minutes for updates, while the dog hadn’t moved in hours.

Two weeks later, everything changed. The first firm withdrew their offer after discovering hidden debt. The second found regulatory issues. The third couldn’t secure financing at reasonable rates. The phone rang. The acquisition target’s CEO, voice strained: “We need to talk. Privately. Today.” The owner looked at his dog, who had lifted his head at the phone’s ring, ears forward, suddenly alert. “Now we move,” the owner said. They acquired the company for sixty percent of the original asking price. Clean terms. No competition. No bidding war.

13 “How did you know?” the director asked later, genuinely puzzled. The owner was filling his dog’s water bowl. The Lab waited patiently for him to finish, staying back until the owner stepped away from the bowl. “Twenty years ago, I would have been first through that door,” the owner said. “Lost a lot of money being first. Now I watch my dog. He waits until waiting becomes the right action.” The director watched the Lab drink slowly, methodically. Unhurried and calm.

“Scripture tells us there’s a time for everything,” the owner continued. “A time to be aggressive and a time to be still. The hardest part isn’t knowing what to do; it’s knowing when to do it.” That evening, as they prepared to leave, the director noticed something. The owner put on his coat and moved toward the door. The dog immediately stood and walked to the exit, then sat down, looking back, waiting for his owner to actually open it.

14 Even leaving was about patience. About reading the moment. About moving only when movement made sense. The director thought about all the deals he’d rushed into. All the opportunities he’d chased because everyone else was chasing them. All the doors he’d tried to force open. Maybe the owner and his old dog understood something he was just beginning to learn: True strength isn’t in the speed of your response. It’s in knowing when response is actually needed. Sometimes the best business strategy looks like an old dog by his owner’s desk: calm, watchful, ready to move when the moment is right, and completely still until then.

In business, as in faith, patience isn’t passive. It’s active trust. It’s watching carefully while others panic. It’s being ready while others rush. It’s knowing that the One who controls all outcomes has perfect timing. The dog had learned it instinctively. Now the director was beginning to understand: Sometimes the most powerful move is to wait. 15

The construction foreman watched his Beagle trot across the job site with his tail wagging in wide circles. The crew had just finished framing the biggest project their small company had ever landed: a three-story medical complex. “We did it!” shouted one of the younger workers, pulling out his phone. “This is going on my LinkedIn right now. Look at what we built!” The foreman set down his coffee and whistled. The Beagle came running, tail still wagging, covered in sawdust but clearly thrilled to be there. Duke had been coming to job sites for many years, same enthusiasm whether they were building a shed or a shopping center.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: “Hold on,” the foreman said to his crew. “Let’s talk about this.” The young architect they’d hired fresh from school had already drafted a press release. She’d taken dozens of photos from dramatic angles. “This will 16 get us noticed. Every construction company in the state will know what we accomplished.” Duke circled the group, his tail maintaining its happy rhythm. He’d been here every day through the permitting delays, through the rain that set them back two weeks, through that foundation problem that nearly killed the project. His tail never stopped wagging.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: “You see that dog?” the foreman asked. “He’s happy about the work, not the credit.” “So?” the architect asked. The foreman had run this company for fifteen years. Started with just him and his brother-in-law doing kitchen remodels. Now he had twelve full- time employees and more work than he could handle. He’d never once taken out an ad. “People need to know what we can do,” the architect insisted. The foreman pointed to the medical complex. “They will. When doctors work here. When patients get better here. When the foundation
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: 17 doesn’t crack and the roof doesn’t leak long after we’re gone.” Duke had found a stick and was carrying it around the site, showing it to each worker. Pure joy over a piece of wood. The same joy he’d shown that morning over breakfast. The same joy he’d show tonight over dinner. The crew watched Duke for a moment. The young worker slowly lowered his phone. Three months later, the building opened quietly. No ribbon cutting. No press coverage. The foreman insisted they skip the fanfare and get to work on their next project.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: But something interesting happened. The doctors in the medical complex started talking. They mentioned the construction crew that showed up early, cleaned up completely, and fixed a design flaw without being asked. The building manager noted that this was the only construction project he’d overseen that finished on time and under budget. Within six months, the foreman’s company had three new contracts. All word of mouth. All from 18 people who’d either worked in or visited the medical complex. “How did they all find out about us?” the architect asked one morning.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: The foreman was filling Duke’s water bowl. The Beagle waited, tail wagging, until the foreman stepped back. “Good work travels,” the foreman said. “Just slower than gossip.” At the next job site, the architect noticed something. When they hit a milestone, Duke’s tail would wag. When they hit a setback, Duke’s tail would still wag. The dog found joy in being there, being part of the work, regardless of the outcome. She stopped taking dramatic photos. Deleted the press release template from her laptop. Started focusing on the blueprints instead of the social media posts.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: A year later, at the company Christmas party, someone mentioned they’d been featured in a regional construction magazine as “the company everyone’s talking about but no one’s heard of.” 19 The foreman raised his beer. “To good work and good people.” Duke wandered through the party, tail constantly moving, greeting everyone with the same enthusiasm. Happy to be there. Happy to be included. Needing no recognition beyond the occasional pat on the head. The architect understood now. Pride announces itself. Joy just radiates. And in the end, the thing that radiates travels further than the thing that shouts.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: Scripture says, “Let someone else praise you, and not your own mouth.” That night, when a client asked the architect about their work, she found herself using the foreman’s words: “Come see what we’ve built.” Duke’s tail kept wagging. Same rhythm. Same joy. Same quiet celebration of work worth doing. 20
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Let joy speak louder than pride.

chunks.text: The restaurant owner’s Portuguese Water Dog lay sprawled across the kitchen’s back entrance, nose twitching even in his sleep. Thursday morning delivery day. Same routine for years. The produce truck pulled up at dawn. The driver wheeled in crates of vegetables while the owner checked inventory. Everything looked perfect. Crisp lettuce. Bright tomatoes. Fresh herbs. The Portuguese Water Dog lifted his head. Stood slowly. His curly black coat rippled as he walked to one particular crate and sat down, refusing to move.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: “Come on, Sailor,” the owner said. “We’ve got work to do.” Sailor didn’t budge. His nose worked over the crate of spinach, then he looked back at the owner with those deep brown eyes, serious and insistent. 21 The driver grew impatient. “Look, I’ve got six more stops. The invoice matches. Everything’s fresh from the farm.” The owner paused, watching Sailor’s insistent posture. She’d seen this only once before, years ago when she was still new to the business. A delivery of seafood that looked perfect. Sailor had done the exact same thing—sat down, wouldn’t budge. She’d ignored him then, too eager to please a difficult customer. That night, three people got sick. Not severely, thank God, but enough to teach her a lesson she never forgot.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: She opened the crate, dug past the top layer. The spinach underneath was starting to turn. Slight discoloration. Barely visible. But if she’d served it, someone could have gotten sick. “Take it back,” she said. “You’re rejecting the whole shipment over one crate?” The driver was angry now. Sailor had moved to another crate. Same behavior. Sat down. Wouldn’t move. 22 The owner checked. Found issues in three more crates. Product that looked fine on top but had problems underneath.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: The driver left angry, threatening to report her to his supervisor. The owner watched him go, then knelt beside Sailor, running her hand through his wavy coat. Portuguese Water Dogs were bred to be fishermen’s helpers, she remembered. Generations of working alongside humans, learning to sense danger, understanding what mattered. Her sous chef had been watching from the doorway. “How did he know?” “He pays attention to what others miss,” the owner said. Two days later, the news broke. Eight restaurants in the city had reported food poisoning cases. All traced back to the same supplier. The same Thursday delivery. The health department issued a recall.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: The owner’s restaurant was the only one on that route that hadn’t served the contaminated produce. 23 “Lucky guess?” the sous chef asked. The owner watched Sailor methodically sniffing around the kitchen, checking every corner, every container. He wasn’t aggressive about it. Wasn’t anxious. Just thorough. Just careful. His breed had worked on fishing boats for centuries, where one mistake could mean disaster. That heritage lived in his bones. “Not luck,” she said. “Love.” The sous chef looked confused. “He’s not protecting the restaurant,” the owner explained. “He’s protecting the people who eat here. There’s a difference.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: Over the years, the owner had learned to read Sailor’s signals. A certain way he’d position himself when a new employee wasn’t trustworthy. How he’d linger by the tables of regular customers who were having hard days, staying close when he’d usually return to his spot. The way he’d become restless when equipment was about to fail. Some called it instinct. The owner knew better. Discernment wasn’t about being suspicious or 24 paranoid. It was about caring enough to notice. Loving enough to pay attention.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: A month later, a new supplier approached them. Better prices. Impressive references. Glossy brochures. The sales rep was smooth. “I guarantee you’ll save twenty percent on your produce costs.” Sailor, who usually slept through meetings, walked over and sat between the owner and the sales rep. His nose worked the air. Then he did something unusual. He turned his back to the sales rep and faced the owner. “Thanks for coming,” the owner said. “We’ll pass.” After the rep left, the sous chef asked, “The dog made that decision?”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: “I made the decision,” the owner corrected. “Sailor just helped me see what I might have missed.” Later that year, the same supplier was investigated for substituting organic labels on conventional produce. Three restaurants faced lawsuits. 25 The owner’s place stayed busy. Customers didn’t come for the ambiance or the trendy menu. They came because they trusted the food. They might not have known about Sailor, sleeping by the kitchen door, his hypoallergenic coat perfect for a restaurant setting, nose always working. But they benefited from his vigilance.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: Scripture tells us to “test everything and hold fast to what is good.” Sailor did that with his nose. The owner did it by trusting Sailor. One evening, a regular customer brought her elderly mother to dinner. The mother was recovering from chemotherapy with a weakened immune system. The owner watched Sailor get up from his usual spot and settle beside their table. He positioned himself between the elderly woman and the busy kitchen traffic, his eyes soft but alert, ready to signal if anything seemed wrong.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: The owner quietly told the kitchen to take extra precautions with that order. Fresh gloves. Separate prep area. Double-check everything. The meal went perfectly. The elderly woman kept it down. First full meal she’d enjoyed in weeks. 26 Discernment isn’t about finding problems. It’s about preventing harm. It’s about loving people enough to notice what might hurt them. Like a Portuguese Water Dog with centuries of working heritage, catching what others miss, sitting down and refusing to move when something’s not right.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: The owner looked at Sailor, back in his spot by the kitchen door. Tomorrow morning, another truck would arrive. Another Thursday delivery. She knew Sailor would be there, nose working, ready to sit down if something wasn’t right. The elderly woman finished her meal, color returning to her face. Her daughter mouthed “thank you” to the owner as they left. The owner scratched behind Sailor’s ears. He’d saved them again today, just like he had with the seafood years ago. Just like he would tomorrow if needed.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: The sous chef was wiping down the prep station. “You know what our real competitive advantage is?” he said. “It’s not our prices or our menu.” 27 The owner looked at Sailor, whose eyes were closing but nose still twitched with each passing scent. “It’s him,” the sous chef said. “People know someone’s looking out for them here. Even if they don’t know it’s a dog.” The owner nodded. That’s what discernment was. Not paranoia. Just love paying attention. 28
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Discernment is a kind of love.

chunks.text: The delivery truck rumbled through the morning fog as the senior driver watched his Australian Shepherd pace behind the seats. Blue had been riding these routes for years, knew every stop, every shortcut, every customer’s name. “First day?” the senior driver asked the trainee behind the wheel. “First time driving,” the trainee said, white- knuckling the steering wheel. “I’ve been riding along for weeks, taking notes. This is my first time actually behind the wheel.” Blue settled between them, eyes fixed on the senior driver, waiting for subtle cues.

“You mapped out the route?” the senior driver asked. “Got it all planned. Reorganized it actually. Your old route wastes time backtracking through downtown. I found a way to save forty minutes.” 29 Blue’s ears perked up as they passed their usual first stop. He looked at the senior driver, who remained quiet. “We just passed our usual first stop,” the trainee said. “But we’ll circle back later. It’s more efficient this way.” The senior driver stayed quiet. Blue’s ears perked up as they passed the familiar storefront.

They drove on. The trainee’s new route looked efficient on paper. Highway most of the way. Fewer turns. Less traffic. But at the third stop, they found the loading dock blocked. Their weekly produce delivery always arrived Tuesday mornings. The senior driver knew this. Blue knew this. They always came here after lunch on Tuesdays. “We’ll have to come back,” the trainee muttered, recalculating. Blue watched the senior driver, who simply nodded. 30 At the fifth stop, they arrived during the restaurant’s lunch rush. No one available to sign. No place to park the truck. Another delay.

“This is frustrating,” the trainee said. The senior driver watched Blue, who had positioned himself to see out the windshield, tracking their location. The dog knew every deviation from the familiar route but stayed silent, eyes on his owner, trusting completely. “Your dog seems anxious,” the trainee observed. “He’s not anxious. He’s paying attention.” By noon, the “improved” route had them an hour behind schedule. The trainee pulled into a parking lot, frustrated. “I don’t understand. This should have worked.”

The senior driver opened the door and Blue hopped out, immediately walking to his owner’s left side, matching his pace perfectly. 31 “Watch him,” the senior driver said as they walked. “All these years, he’s never needed a leash. Know why?” The trainee shook his head. “He learned that staying close is better than running ahead. He could scout every smell, chase every bird. But he’d miss where we’re actually going.” Blue demonstrated this perfectly, pausing when the senior driver paused, turning when he turned, never more than two feet away.

“The route I’ve been running looks inefficient,” the senior driver continued. “But I know when each dock is clear. When businesses take deliveries. Which streets flood when it rains. When school zones are active.” They got back in the truck. The trainee looked at the route map, then at the senior driver. “Could you drive the rest?” The senior driver took the wheel. Blue immediately relaxed, curling up between the seats. 32 As they pulled out, the trainee noticed the dog had completely relaxed, curling up between the seats with slow, easy breathing.

They hit every stop in perfect timing. Dock workers were ready. Signatures were quick. Traffic gaps appeared exactly when needed. “How do you know all this?” the trainee asked. “I spent a long time following drivers who knew more. Now I get to pass that on.” At a red light, Blue stood up, tail wagging. The senior driver smiled. “Miller’s hardware store is next. Blue loves Miller. Gives him treats.” Sure enough, around the corner was Miller’s. The trainee hadn’t even seen it on the map.

“I mapped the most efficient route,” the trainee said quietly. “Shortest distances, least traffic.” “You mapped the streets,” the senior driver said. “But the route isn’t just streets. It’s people. It’s timing. It’s knowing that Miller takes lunch at 33 one, that the school gets out at three-fifteen, that the bank processes deliveries faster before noon, that the loading dock at the mall gets crowded after two.” The trainee looked at his abandoned route map. “They didn’t teach this in my logistics degree.”

“Can’t teach it in a classroom. You learn it by following someone who already knows. By making mistakes when the dock is blocked. By sitting in traffic you could have avoided.” “Feels like I wasted a lot of time in school,” the trainee said. “Not wasted. Just incomplete. Watch Blue when we get out.” At Miller’s, Blue hopped down and immediately went to the senior driver’s left side. When a cat ran across the parking lot, Blue’s muscles tensed but he didn’t chase after it. When another dog barked from behind a fence, Blue’s ears swiveled but he didn’t respond.

34 “Every instinct tells him to react,” the senior driver said. “But he knows his job is to stay with me. That’s not weakness. That’s discipline.” Miller came out with a biscuit for Blue and signatures for the packages. “You training someone new?” Miller asked. “Yup.” the senior driver said. Back in the truck, the trainee took the wheel again but waited for directions instead of following his plan. Back in the truck, the senior driver took them through the alley behind the bank.

“Saves five minutes this time of day,” he explained. Blue watched out the windshield, tail wagging slightly. He recognized the route now. The right route. The one he’d learned over the years. By day’s end, they’d made all their deliveries despite the morning’s setbacks. The trainee’s reorganized route sat folded on the dashboard where he’d tossed it hours ago. 35 “I have a degree in logistics,” the trainee admitted. “Thought I could apply what I learned, save some time, maybe cut costs.”

“You probably can, eventually. But first you follow. You learn why things are done certain ways. Then you can make real improvements, ones that actually work and stick.” Blue was asleep between them now, completely at peace. Years of following the same routes, the same person, the same patterns. Not because he couldn’t think for himself, but because he’d learned that wisdom comes from walking beside someone who knows the way. Scripture says, “Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.” Simple. Direct. Follow first, understand later.

The trainee looked at Blue sleeping peacefully between them. The dog had spent the whole day following, watching, trusting. And they’d ended up exactly where they needed to be. “Same route tomorrow?” the trainee asked. 36 “Same route,” the senior driver confirmed. “Until you know it well enough to teach someone else.” Blue’s tail thumped once against the seat. He understood the wisdom of learning before improving. The trainee was beginning to understand it too. 37

The retail store manager watched her Rottweiler move through the aisles. Tank had learned to navigate the store carefully, all hundred and twenty pounds of him weaving between displays without knocking anything over. “Is that dog safe in here?” a customer asked nervously, clutching her purse. “Safer with him than without him,” the manager replied. It was Saturday afternoon, always the busiest. The store was packed. Holiday shoppers, weekend browsers, teenagers hanging out. And in the middle of it all was the new cashier. Still in high school. This was her first job, and her first time handling the Saturday rush.

The line grew longer. Customers grew impatient. The girl’s hands started shaking as she miscounted change for the third time. 38 “This is ridiculous,” a man in a suit said loudly. “Where’s the real staff?” Tank, who’d been lying by the manager’s office, stood up. He walked slowly to the register and positioned himself between the angry customer and the young cashier, solid and calm. The man took a step back. “Sorry,” the manager said, appearing from the back room. “We’re training someone new. Tank here is just making sure she has space to learn.”

The teenage cashier’s breathing slowed. With Tank standing there like a wall between her and the growing line, she found her rhythm. The register started working properly. The line moved. “Your dog always do that?” an older woman asked, watching Tank maintain his position. “Only when someone needs protection,” the manager said. Later that afternoon, a group of boys came in. The boys were loud and rowdy, making the store feel unsafe. They followed the teenage cashier when 39 she went to stock shelves. Making comments. Laughing when she dropped things.

Tank appeared around the corner. Again, no aggression. He simply walked beside the girl as she worked. When she stopped, he stopped. When she moved, he moved. A hundred and twenty pounds staying right beside her. The boys found somewhere else to be. “I was so scared,” the cashier admitted later, near tears. “Tank knew,” the manager said. “That’s why he showed up when he did.” The manager had bought Tank as a puppy, right after she’d been robbed at her previous job. Everyone expected her to train him as a guard dog. Attack commands. Aggression training. The works.

Instead, she’d trained him to read situations. To position himself between threat and vulnerable. To use his size as a shield, not a weapon. 40 A month earlier, during inventory, an elderly employee had fallen in the stockroom. Tank had immediately lain down beside him, letting the man use his solid body to sit up, then stand. Tank stayed quiet and still, being what was needed: something stable to lean on. “People see a Rottweiler and think violence,” the manager told the cashier as they closed that evening. “But real strength isn’t about attacking. It’s about knowing when someone needs protection and quietly providing it.”

Tank was doing his final walk-through, checking each aisle before closing. He paused at the front door, watching the parking lot as the cashier walked to her car. “He won’t move until you’re safely driving away,” the manager explained. “Why does he care so much?” The manager thought about it. “Because that’s what strength is for. To make sure the vulnerable are safe.” 41 The next Saturday, the teenage cashier was back at the register. The lines were long again. The customers impatient again. But this time, she wasn’t alone. Tank had taken up his position nearby, lying down but alert, ready to stand between her and anyone who might make her feel small.

A mother with three young children came through the line. The youngest, maybe four years old, was crying, overwhelmed by the noise and crowds. Without being called, Tank stood and walked to the child. He sat down at the perfect height for her small hands to reach his head. The crying stopped. Her small fingers had found his soft ears. “Gentle giant,” the mother said, smiling. The manager nodded. That’s exactly what he was. Tank used his strength to comfort and protect people. He didn’t need to prove anything or show off.

Scripture says the strong ought to bear with the weak. Tank lived it every day. He could have 42 dominated any space he entered. Instead, he made himself a shelter. By closing time, the teenage cashier had handled her second Saturday successfully. As she counted down her register, Tank sat beside her, patient and protective. “I used to be scared of big dogs,” she told the manager. “Most people are. They see the size and assume danger.” “But he’s the opposite,” the cashier said. “He makes me feel safe.”

The manager looked at Tank, who was now watching the parking lot again, making sure everyone got to their cars safely. His massive head turned slowly, tracking each person’s movement. That’s what real strength looked like. A shelter, always ready to stand between danger and those who needed protection. 43

The freelance consultant typed another line of code while her Basset Hound slept on the office floor. Winston had been in the same spot all morning. Hadn’t moved except to occasionally shift positions. “Must be nice,” she muttered. Winston opened one eye, looked at her, then closed it and went back to sleep. For weeks, she’d been working from dawn until long after dark. Three clients with overlapping deadlines. A startup that needed their app fixed yesterday. A corporation demanding complex reports with impossible deadlines. Everyone needed everything immediately.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: Her phone buzzed. Another emergency email. Another client who’d forgotten to mention a critical requirement until the night before launch. Winston rolled onto his back, paws in the air, completely relaxed. 44 “You’re not helping,” she told him. The next morning, she could barely get out of bed. Her back hurt. Her eyes burned. Her hands were cramping from typing. Winston waddled over, looked at her struggling to sit up, then walked to his food bowl and waited. “Right. You need breakfast.” She forced herself up.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: After feeding him, she sat back at her desk. The emails had multiplied overnight. Dozens of new messages. Each one urgent. Winston finished eating, walked to his bed in the sunny spot by the window, circled three times, and lay down. Within minutes, he was asleep again. She looked at him fondly. “How do you do it? How can you sleep so much?” But as she watched, she noticed something. When the doorbell rang with a delivery, Winston was instantly alert, checking who was there. When it was safe, he went back to sleep. When she got up to get coffee, he watched until she sat down, then closed his eyes again. He wasn’t lazy. He was conserving energy for when it mattered.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: 45 That afternoon, she hit a wall. The code wasn’t working. The reports made no sense. She’d been staring at the same error message for too long. Winston got up, stretched extensively, and walked to the back door. He needed to go out. She followed him outside. The sun was shining. She hadn’t noticed. Winston sniffed around the yard, taking his time. She sat on the porch steps. The sun felt warm on her face. She closed her eyes, and for the first time in days, actually felt the warmth. Saw the orange glow through her eyelids. Her mind, usually racing with code and deadlines, went quiet for a moment.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: Her neighbor waved from his garden. “Haven’t seen you in a while.” “Been busy.” He looked at Winston, who had found a patch of sun and was lying down again. “Your dog’s got the right idea. Perfect day for a rest.” “I don’t have time to rest.” 46 “Nobody has time to rest. That’s why you have to make time to rest.” Back inside, she looked at her computer. The error message was still there. But now she saw the problem. A simple typo she’d been too exhausted to notice. Fixed immediately.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: She realized she’d been making more mistakes lately. Taking twice as long to do simple tasks. Working harder but accomplishing less. Winston was back in his spot, snoring softly. She thought about something her grandmother used to say: “Even God rested on the seventh day, and He wasn’t tired.” Her phone rang. Another client emergency. “I can look at it tomorrow,” she heard herself say. “Tomorrow? But we need—“ “Tomorrow. I’ll be fresh and can fix it properly. Or if you need to have it done by tonight, I suggest finding someone else.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: 47 She hung up. Closed her laptop. Winston opened one eye, watching her. “You’re right,” she told him. “Rest isn’t what you do when the work is done. It’s what you do so the work can get done.” She lay down on the couch. Winston waddled over and curled up on the floor beside her. For the first time in weeks, she slept. Really slept. She’d left the laptop at her desk, put her phone in airplane mode, and literally fell asleep within minutes.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: When she woke, it was dark. Winston was still there, though he’d moved to catch the last bit of warmth from the heating vent. She checked her phone. Missed calls and emails had piled up. She ordered pizza instead of opening the laptop. The next morning, she set new rules. Work started in the morning. Ended in the evening. Winston would get real walks, not just quick bathroom breaks. Three times a day: morning, lunch, and 48 evening. Lunch break midday, whether she was hungry or not. Weekends off.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: “Like a real job,” she said to Winston. He was already in his morning sunny spot, eyes closed. The clients complained at first. Threatened to find someone else. Two actually did. But something interesting happened. The work she did produce was better. Fewer errors. Cleaner code. Clearer reports. The clients who stayed respected her boundaries, received better results, and started paying her more. Months later, she was making more money working fewer hours. Winston’s routine hadn’t changed. Sleep, eat, brief walks, more sleep. But she’d learned to match his rhythm. Work when working. Rest when resting. No guilt either way.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: A new client called, demanding a rush job over the weekend. “I don’t work weekends,” she said. “This is urgent!” 49 She looked at Winston, deeply asleep in his afternoon spot. “Everything’s urgent until it’s not. I’ll look at it Monday.” “But—“ “Monday.” She hung up. Winston’s tail thumped once against the floor, though his eyes stayed closed. Scripture says, “Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.” Winston lived it. Rest wasn’t his reward for being a good dog. It was how he stayed a good dog.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Rest is not the reward — it's the rhythm.

chunks.text: The farmer watched his Jack Russell Terrier frozen at the barn door. Pepper had been sitting there for a while. “What’s he waiting for?” the banker asked, checking his watch. He’d come to discuss the loan for next season’s crops. “The right moment,” the farmer said. There was a rat in the barn. The farmer knew it, and so did Pepper. But the terrier hadn’t moved; he just watched and waited. The banker shifted impatiently. “We really need to discuss your crop rotation plans. Corn prices are up, so you should plant your entire acreage. The opportunity won’t last.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: The farmer kept his eyes on Pepper. “Sometimes opportunities aren’t what they seem.” For generations, his family had grown diverse crops: corn, soybeans, and wheat. Some years corn 51 paid best. Other years, soybeans saved them. His grandfather’s rule was simple: never put everything into one crop, no matter how good the price looked. But corn was at record highs. Every farmer in the county was converting to corn only. The banker had the numbers to prove it would triple profits.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: Something moved in the barn. Pepper’s ears swiveled forward, but he still didn’t move. “Your neighbors have already committed,” the banker pressed. “Everyone’s going all corn this year.” The farmer remembered another time everyone went the same direction. The market crashed. Half the farms in the county went under. Suddenly, Pepper exploded into motion. He moved with pure speed and purpose. He had the rat in an instant. It was quick and clean. The banker jumped. “I didn’t even see him move.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: “That’s because you were watching me, not him,” the farmer said. “Pepper knew that rat would 52 eventually have to run for food. He just had to wait for the right moment.” The terrier trotted back with his tail high. Having finished the job, he dropped the rat by the barn door and sat, looking up at the farmer for approval. “Good boy,” the farmer said, then turned to the banker. “I’ll plant corn on thirty percent of my land, soybeans on another thirty, keep thirty in wheat, and leave ten percent fallow.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: “That’s leaving money on the table.” “That’s staying in business when the market turns.” The banker pulled out his spreadsheets, showing projected profits from going all corn. The numbers were compelling. The farmer could pay off his equipment, maybe even buy the adjacent land he’d been eyeing. Pepper had found a sunny spot and curled up for a nap. His work was done. He’d waited for exactly the right moment, acted decisively, then rested. 53 “Your dog just proved my point,” the banker said. “He saw the opportunity and took it. Fast and complete.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: “He waited all that time to act in an instant,” the farmer corrected. “And he only caught that rat because he knew its patterns. Rush in too early, and the rat goes right back in. Wait too long, it gets its food and goes back into the walls.” That afternoon, the farmer walked his fields with Pepper running ahead, investigating every interesting smell. The soil was good and the weather forecast was favorable. Everything said this would be a profitable year for corn.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: But the farmer had learned something from generations of farming and from watching his Jack Russell work: the difference between motion and progress, between opportunity and trap. His phone rang. Another neighbor calling to say he was going all corn, asking if the farmer wanted to coordinate planting to get better prices on seed. “I’m sticking with my rotation,” the farmer said. “You’re going to miss out.” 54 The farmer watched Pepper investigate a rabbit hole, patient and methodical. The terrier could have gone in headfirst but he didn’t. He circled, sniffed, assessed. Then moved on. Not every hole was worth pursuing.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: When October came, the corn harvest was massive. The oversupply crashed prices, and farmers who’d gone all corn couldn’t cover their loans. But wheat and soybean prices held steady. Because the farmer had diversified, he stayed profitable while others scrambled. The banker returned that winter. He had a different tune this time. “Several farms are going into foreclosure. There might be land available if you’re interested.” Pepper was at his post by the barn door again, watching something the humans couldn’t see yet.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: “What’s he tracking now?” the banker asked. “Next season’s opportunities,” the farmer said. “He’ll let me know when it’s time to move.” The banker shook his head. “You really make decisions based on what your dog does?” 55 “I make decisions based on patience and timing. Pepper just reminds me what that looks like.” Spring came. The farmers who survived the corn crash were gun-shy, planting too conservatively. But the farmer had been preparing through winter. When the moment came to buy that adjacent land at foreclosure prices, he was ready. He made a quick decision with clean execution. Just like Pepper with the rat.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: “Wisdom isn’t knowing when to run,” he told his son, who was learning the business. “It’s knowing when to wait and when waiting turns into the right time to move.” Pepper was teaching the same lesson to a young stray dog that had shown up at the farm. The younger dog wanted to chase everything immediately. Pepper would hold him back with his body, making him wait, showing him the difference between frantic activity and purposeful action. Scripture says there’s a time for everything. A time to plant and a time to harvest. A time to wait and a time to run.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: 56 Pepper understood. After years of watching, the farmer understood too. And hopefully, his son would understand before it was his turn to decide when to move and when to be still. The banker left with the loan approved for the farmer’s conservative but proven strategy. As his car pulled away, Pepper suddenly sprinted across the yard, launching himself at something invisible to human eyes. Another successful catch. “All that waiting for a moment of action,” the farmer’s son observed.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Wisdom knows when to move.

chunks.text: The veterinary technician watched her Cocker Spaniel navigate the clinic’s back entrance, golden fur catching the morning light. Honey had been coming to work with her for years as a calming presence for nervous patients. ”Is that your dog?” the new vet asked, fresh from veterinary school. “She’s our unofficial therapy dog,” the tech replied. “She knows this place better than most of the staff.” It had been a rough morning. They’d had a difficult euthanasia, followed by an emergency surgery that didn’t go as planned. The new vet was taking it hard, second-guessing every decision she’d made.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: Honey sensed the tension. She walked over to the new vet and sat beside her, leaning gently against her leg. 58 “I should have caught that complication earlier,” the vet said, slumping in her chair. “In school, we practiced this procedure hundreds of times.” “School isn’t real life,” the tech said. “Real animals don’t read the textbooks.” Just then, a cat escaped from an exam room, racing through the clinic with its owner in pursuit. Honey, who usually ignored the clinic cats, couldn’t resist the chase instinct. She bolted after the cat, skidding on the freshly mopped floor, crashing into a cart of supplies that scattered everywhere.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: The cat made it safely back to its owner. Honey stood in the middle of the mess, tail tucked, ears down, looking mortified. “Honey!” the tech sighed. But instead of scolding, she laughed. “You’re usually so good, and you pick today to chase a cat?” She helped clean up the supplies while Honey watched, still looking embarrassed. Once everything was back in place, the tech knelt beside her dog. 59 “It’s okay, girl. We all make mistakes.” Honey’s tail started wagging again. It was tentative at first, then gained confidence. Within minutes, she was back to her usual self, greeting the next patient with enthusiasm.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: The new vet watched this interaction. “She just… moved on. Like it never happened.” “That’s what she does,” the tech said. “Yesterday she accidentally knocked over a whole tray of vaccines, expensive ones. I was frustrated, sure, but what good would it do to dwell on it? She learned to be more careful around the vaccine fridge, and we moved on.” Later that afternoon, a family brought in their elderly Golden Retriever. The dog was anxious and whimpering, clearly in pain. The new vet examined him carefully.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: “I think it’s bone cancer,” she said quietly to the tech. “We should do x-rays to confirm.” The x-rays revealed the truth. It was advanced osteosarcoma, the aggressive bone cancer common in larger dogs. The family was devastated. 60 “Could we have caught it earlier?” the mother asked through tears. The new vet started to speak, probably to list all the signs they might have missed, but the tech interrupted gently. “What matters is that you’re here now, and we can help him be comfortable. You’re doing the right thing.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: After the family left with pain medication and a follow-up appointment to discuss options, the new vet sat heavily in the break room. “I wanted to tell them the truth. That if they’d brought him in earlier when he first started limping…” “And what would that accomplish? Make them feel guilty on top of grieving?” The tech shook her head. “They’re already carrying enough.” Honey wandered into the break room, her usual mid-afternoon routine. She’d learned that someone usually dropped crumbs during lunch. Today, she found nothing, but instead of leaving disappointed, she curled up at the new vet’s feet.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: 61 “I had a professor who never let us forget our mistakes,” the new vet said. “He said it would make us better vets.” “Did it?” “No. It just made us afraid to try.” The tech nodded. “I’ve been doing this for years. You want to know the secret to lasting in this field?” The new vet looked up. “Learn from your mistakes, then let them go. Like Honey with that cat. She’ll probably never chase another cat in the clinic. But she’s not going to spend the rest of her life feeling guilty about it either.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: A week later, the new vet faced another emergency surgery. It had a similar presentation to the one that had gone wrong. Her hands shook as she scrubbed in. Honey was in her usual spot in the corner of the prep room, watching. The tech noticed the vet’s nervousness. 62 “You’ve got this,” she said simply. The surgery went perfectly. The vet caught a potential complication early, adjusted her approach, and saved the dog’s life. “Nice work,” the senior vet said afterward. “That was a tricky one.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: The new vet found Honey in the recovery room, lying beside the waking patient. “You know what I learned?” she told the tech later. “That first surgery that went wrong? It taught me what to watch for. If I hadn’t made that mistake, I might not have caught the problem today.” The tech smiled. “Mistakes are just experience in disguise.” Months passed. The new vet grew more confident. She still made mistakes, as everyone did, but she’d learned to acknowledge them, learn from them, and move forward.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: One day, a new veterinary student started shadowing at the clinic. During her first day, she accidentally gave a cat the wrong vaccine. It wasn’t 63 harmful, but it meant the cat would need to come back for the correct one. The student was mortified, nearly in tears. The new vet, now not so new, found her hiding in the supply room. “I’m so stupid,” the student said. “I can’t believe I did that.” Honey appeared, as she always did when someone was upset. She pushed her head under the student’s hand, demanding pets.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: “Let me tell you about my first emergency surgery here,” the vet said. “And about a dog who taught me that mistakes don’t have to define us.” She told the story while the student petted Honey. The dog had rolled onto her back for belly rubs, completely at ease. “The client wasn’t angry?” the student asked about the vaccine mix-up. “We explained, apologized, and offered to cover the return visit. They understood. People are more 64 forgiving than we expect, especially when we own our mistakes honestly.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: The clinic’s philosophy had become clear over time: excellence wasn’t about perfection. It was about learning, growing, and not letting fear of failure prevent them from trying. Honey demonstrated this daily. She’d learned not to chase cats in the clinic, but she still played with them at home. She’d learned to avoid the vaccine fridge, but still checked the break room for crumbs. Each mistake had taught her something without diminishing her spirit. “Grace,” the tech said one day, watching Honey comfort a scared puppy getting its first shots. “That’s what she has. Grace to forgive herself and others.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: The new vet, now mentoring the student, understood. In veterinary medicine, as in life, you’d make mistakes. Some small, some significant. The difference between those who thrived and those who burned out wasn’t skill or intelligence. It was the ability to accept grace: for yourself and others. 65 Honey aged over the years but never lost her ability to shake off the bad moments. Even on her last day at the clinic, when arthritis made her movements slow, she knocked over a water bowl, looked embarrassed for a moment, then wagged her tail and moved on.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: That’s what stuck with everyone who worked there. What mattered was not the mistakes, but the recovery, not the failures, but the grace to try again. Scripture says we all fall short, but grace covers our shortcomings. Honey lived that truth every day, teaching a clinic full of perfectionists that healing, whether for animals or humans, began with forgiving ourselves. The clinic kept her collar hanging in the break room after she passed. Below it, a small sign: “Mistakes happen. Grace happens more.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Grace means we don't have to stay stuck.

chunks.text: Three sled dogs worked at a small ranch in Montana. They were regular working dogs, the kind that haul loads day after day. The lead dog was older but steady, the middle dog was younger and stronger, and the third was the smallest but she watched everything. One winter afternoon, dark clouds rolled in from the north. The farmer looked at the sky, then at the upper field where hay bales sat waiting. If the snow hit before he got them down to the barn, the animals would go hungry for weeks.

He tried hitching up the lead dog alone first. She knew the route and pulled with everything she had, but she couldn’t move it. She stopped, exhausted. “Alright, girl,” the farmer said. “Let’s try something different.” He hitched all three dogs together. They wrestled at the start, bumping into each other while getting into position. They moved at different speeds 67 naturally. The young dog wanted to charge ahead while the small dog kept stopping to look around. The old dog waited patiently for them to settle.

But once they were hitched together, they found their rhythm. The old dog set the pace, steady and sure, while the young dog pulled hardest when the sled hit deep snow. When they started to drift, the small dog corrected their course, and when the old dog’s strength flagged halfway down, she somehow sensed it and moved forward to take more weight. They worked as one, and the sled moved. They got all the bales down to the barn before the first snowflakes fell.

The farmer unhitched them and stood watching as they drank from the water trough together. None of them could have pulled that load alone. The old dog was too weak, the young dog had no sense of direction, and the small dog had no power. But together? Together they’d done what seemed impossible. That night, after the storm had buried the upper field under deep snow, the farmer sat in his kitchen thinking about those three dogs. He thought 68 about his own work, the way he’d been trying to run the ranch alone since his brother moved away. The way he’d been telling himself it was faster to do everything himself. The way he’d been slowly drowning under the weight of it all.

The next morning, he made a phone call. He asked for help and admitted he couldn’t carry it alone. Because sometimes the wisest thing we can do is stay with the pack. Even when it’s messy. Even when others move at different speeds. Even when we have to adjust our rhythm to match theirs. Scripture says it plainly: “Two are better than one, because they have a good return for their labor. If either of them falls down, one can help the other up.”

The three dogs knew this without being told. They knew that what matters isn’t being the strongest or the fastest or the smartest. What matters is staying together, adjusting to each other’s pace, and carrying each other when the load gets too heavy. Because together, we go farther than we ever could alone. 69

Atlas was a German Shepherd guide dog who worked downtown, navigating busy streets and crowded sidewalks every day. He had been trained for years, and part of that training taught him something most dogs never learn: how to hold back. One afternoon, Atlas and his handler were crossing the street when a delivery driver leaned out his window and shouted. The truck revved its engine and rolled forward into the crosswalk while they were still in it. Atlas’s muscles tensed. His ears went back. Every instinct told him to bark, to warn this threat away. He could have made the driver stop with a single growl.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.

chunks.text: But Atlas held still. He guided his handler safely across while the shouting continued behind them. Then they kept walking. The next week at the same intersection, a teenager on a skateboard came barreling through and nearly 70 knocked the handler over. The kid grabbed the man’s jacket to catch his balance, which startled Atlas. The dog turned quickly, teeth showing for just a moment. The teenager jumped back and skated away fast. Atlas stayed alert. He had wanted to protect. He had wanted to act. But he didn’t bite. He had chosen restraint even when everything in him said to strike.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.

chunks.text: A month later, they were at their regular coffee shop when another dog lunged at Atlas while barking and snarling. The smaller dog kept snapping, trying to get closer to Atlas despite its owner’s attempts to control it. Atlas could have ended it in one second. He outweighed the other dog three times over. One bite and the threat would be gone. Instead, Atlas backed up slowly and positioned himself between his handler and the aggressive dog. He stood still. His body was tense and ready, but he didn’t growl. He didn’t snap back. He waited until the other owner finally got control and dragged their dog away.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.

chunks.text: 71 Then Atlas and his handler walked on. This happened again and again over the years. Atlas was threatened, bumped, yelled at, even attacked by other dogs. Each time, he had the power to strike back. Each time, he had reason to defend himself. Each time, he chose not to. Because Atlas understood something that takes most creatures a lifetime to learn. Strength isn’t proved by using it. Real strength is knowing when to hold back, when to stay calm, when to refuse to let someone else’s aggression pull you into becoming aggressive too.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.

chunks.text: Scripture says it plainly: “Do not repay evil with evil. Overcome evil with good.” Atlas knew this without being told. When threatened, the most powerful response isn’t always the one that strikes back. Sometimes it’s the one that stands firm, holds position, and simply refuses to bite. Because in the end, who we’re becoming matters. 72
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Restraint is stronger than revenge.

chunks.text: Sky was a Golden Retriever who lived with an elderly woman in a small cottage. Every morning at six, Sky would sit by his bowl in the kitchen and wait. He never paced. He never whined. He never searched the floor for crumbs or tried to open the pantry door. He simply sat and waited. The woman’s routine varied each day. Sometimes she fed him at six-fifteen. Sometimes not until seven. On difficult mornings when her arthritis flared up, it might be even later. But Sky never panicked. He waited with quiet confidence because he knew she would feed him. She always had.

One winter, things got harder. When the woman’s pension check was delayed, money grew tight. Sky’s food supply dwindled until there was only enough for a few more days. 73 The woman worried. She made phone calls and visited the bank. She checked her mailbox twice a day. She lay awake at night calculating what she had left, wondering how she would feed herself and her dog. Sky sat by his bowl each morning at six. He stayed calm and kept trusting.

On the day they ran out of food, the woman sat at her kitchen table and cried. Sky walked over and rested his head on her knee. She looked down at him, this dog who had no idea about pension checks or bank accounts or bills, and yet somehow remained peaceful. That afternoon, a neighbor knocked on the door. She had bought too much dog food by mistake, she said. Would Sky like some? The woman accepted it gratefully, tears filling her eyes again.

Two days later, the delayed pension check finally arrived. Through all of it, Sky had simply trusted. He had no backup plan. He couldn’t work for food or save money or call anyone for help. His entire security 74 rested on one person. And she had never failed him. Years later, the woman would tell people about that winter. She would say that Sky taught her something she had somehow forgotten. She had run her own small business for decades: a seamstress shop where she managed every detail, tracked every penny, and built security through careful planning. These were all good things, skills that served her well. But somewhere along the way she had started believing that her provision came from her own careful management rather than from the One who provides.

Sky knew better. He understood what she was still learning. Scripture says it plainly: “Give us this day our daily bread.” Give us this day our daily bread: today’s provision, not tomorrow’s worry. Because the One who feeds the birds and clothes the flowers has never once forgotten His own. 75 Sky sat by his bowl every morning for years. Some mornings the food came early. Some mornings it came late. But it always came. Because provision doesn’t come from the paycheck or the pension or the deal we’re counting on. It comes from the Provider who has never missed a single day.

76 Final Thoughts Good business begins with a faithful heart. Business is more than numbers and plans and products. It’s people. It’s choices. It’s moments where character is revealed and where trust is either built or broken. Every deal, every deadline, every delay gives us a chance to show who we belong to. The world tells us to compete, to climb, to hustle harder, to push past people and chase the win. But good dogs remind us of something simpler: faithfulness, loyalty, stillness, and trust.

They show up, they listen, they guard, they forgive, they follow. They stay close to us. And in those small, quiet acts, they point to something greater. Someone greater. We walk with the same God who clothes the flowers and feeds the birds. He walks with us through boardrooms and job sites, offices and kitchens, early mornings and late nights. 77 God has not forgotten us. We are not left to carry this alone. Whether we’re leading companies or folding towels, starting over or holding on, may we work with the kind of faith that trusts the Master, loves the pack, and stays ready to move when He calls.

78 Meet The Authors Kyung Suk Yi is, most importantly, a Christian who has been faithfully following his Lord and Savior Jesus Christ for over 40 years. He started his computer and software business in Silicon Valley in 1982 and continues to humbly Painting of Kyung Suk Yi learn what it means to be and Mola by Kim Yun Hwa a Christ-centered business owner. God has blessed Kyung with an obedient, empathetic heart and numerous opportunities to bring the compassion and truth of God to the elderly, Native Americans, the homeless, incarcerated individuals, and others who suffer.

Kyung also loves dogs. Through decades of observing hundreds of dogs, he has written an equal number of articles and has published numerous books, including Cry of Caleb (2004), Cry of Mola (2008), Cry of Balto (2009), Cry of 79 Cynics (2010), Cry of Sirius (2011), and Cry of Jindo (2023). Now, through a collaborative effort with Jin Sung Kim, he has started publishing a new book series called Tails of Grace. This series of books are about how good dogs encourage us to learn God’s truth and love our God.

Jin graduated from the University of California at Berkeley with a B.A. in Peace And Conflict Studies. Then went on to the Peter F. Drucker and Masatoshi Ito Graduate School of Management at the Claremont Graduate University and earned his M.B.A. with a dual concentration in marketing and strategy. Loyalty to his birth country led him to serve in the Republic of Korea Army, 2nd Division, Artillery Regiment, 280th Battalion in logistics. Upon returning to the U.S. he worked in marketing for LG. Out of the blue, he also had the opportunity to be the founding professor for the business school at Oikos University. But all of that does not matter; what matters is that Jin is a Christian, a sinner who has been forgiven much, doing his best to love the Lord and his neighbor, one one day at a time.
